b' Rahm\xe2\x80\x99s Recs, The Battle of the Johns & Stefanik Second-Order Effects
Welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. In tonight\xe2\x80\x99s issue, Democrats confront a troubling reality after last week\xe2\x80\x99s Trump beatdown: burnout, fatigue, and finger-pointing. There are no hints of a #Resistance 2.0 on the horizon, no obvious savior on the sidelines, as Democrats worry that their voters are going to check out just as Trump gets to work. So how can they come back? I surveyed Democratic leaders\xe2\x80\x94including a feisty Rahm Emanuel in Tokyo\xe2\x80\x94who say it\xe2\x80\x99s time to punch left and be honest about why the party has lost touch with the average Joe and Jane. \xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c

Welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. I\xe2\x80\x99m Peter Hamby , reporting from Los Angeles as Trump \xe2\x80\x99s cabinet nominations begin to roll in: Susie Wiles for chief of staff; Elise Stefanik as ambassador to the U.N.; the baffling selection of Lee Zeldin to lead the E.P.A., despite having never demonstrated any interest in the environment or protecting it; and now, potentially, Marco Rubio as secretary of state, assuming Trump doesn\xe2\x80\x99t change his mind at the last minute.

In tonight\xe2\x80\x99s issue, Democrats confront a troubling reality after last week\xe2\x80\x99s Trump beatdown: burnout, fatigue, and finger-pointing. There are no hints of a #Resistance 2.0 on the horizon, no obvious savior on the sidelines, as Democrats worry that their voters are going to check out just as Trump gets to work. So how can they come back? I surveyed Democratic leaders\xe2\x80\x94including a feisty Rahm Emanuel in Tokyo\xe2\x80\x94who say it\xe2\x80\x99s time to punch left and be honest about why the party has lost touch with the average Joe and Jane.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

\xf0\x9f\x8e\xa7 The CNN reckoning : If you missed today\xe2\x80\x99s episode of The Powers That Be , where Jon Kelly and I took a stab at dissecting a few of the more interesting plot points emerging from last week, you can stream the episode here . We also dig into CNN\xe2\x80\x99s impending layoffs, and whether The Washington Post \xe2\x80\x99s return-to-work policy is really just a game of 4D chess from leadership hoping to tame the ranks.

Now, here\xe2\x80\x99s Abby Livingston on the chaos already engulfing the Hill\xe2\x80\xa6

The Johns\xe2\x80\x99 Bannon Battle & Johnson\xe2\x80\x99s Stefanik Pickle
Congress returns to the Capitol this week, and it\xe2\x80\x99s back to the Trump reality show and the familiar question: How much pressure will the once and future president exert on Capitol Hill? While much of the media is consumed by post-election navel-gazing and 2028 catastrophizing, Trump\xe2\x80\x99s MAGA allies are barreling ahead and already showing a propensity to piss off the Senate in ways they didn\xe2\x80\x99t dare during Trump\xe2\x80\x99s first rodeo. The president-elect, meanwhile, is running roughshod over the Republicans\xe2\x80\x99 narrow margin in the House as he looks to staff up his administration. Here are some emerging themes\xe2\x80\xa6

  • Bannon bullies the Senate : MAGA warriors spent the last day or so arm-twisting Republican senators in a bid to garner support for Florida\xe2\x80\x99s Rick Scott as Senate majority leader, a move that would bypass The Johns , Thune and Cornyn , who have worked meticulously for years to cultivate relationships with colleagues in preparation for this moment. Acknowledging the secret ballot selection process, Steve Bannon and others are demanding a public vote, which is anathema to basically every leadership race on the Hill, outside of a speaker\xe2\x80\x99s floor vote.

    Most G.O.P. senators, of course, hate this idea. Will they stand up to Bannon\xe2\x80\x99s pressure and set a small but substantive precedent of pushback from the Hill against Trump allies? It\xe2\x80\x99s a safe bet, regardless, that MAGA voters will be calling these offices, demanding the senators reveal who they support, which could translate into angry constituents. (Thoughts and prayers for Senate Republican staff assistants manning the phones today.) On the other hand, if MAGA prevails in this push for a loyalty oath, prepare for a whole new ball game on Capitol Hill.

  • Manic Monday in the House : The speed with which Trump plucked Elise Stefanik to be U.S. ambassador to the United Nations caught some members off guard, even though it was common knowledge she was angling for an administration post. What was even more startling on Monday evening was Kaitlan Collins \xe2\x80\x99 report that Trump was also plucking Florida Rep. Mike Waltz from the House to serve as National Security Advisor. Because that post doesn\xe2\x80\x99t require Senate confirmation, this will be an early vacancy. As late as Friday, it seemed impossible to House insiders that Trump would mine their talent pool, due to the tight margin coming out of this election. More of the outstanding races have fallen Republicans\xe2\x80\x99 way, but every one of these appointments creates a more stressful G.O.P. headcount. Waltz, at least, represents a safe Republican seat, and will likely be replaced by a fellow conservative.

    Stefanik\xe2\x80\x99s exit, however, will have greater repercussions, setting off a race for her conference chair leadership post, with Michigan\xe2\x80\x99s Lisa McClain , Florida\xe2\x80\x99s Kat Cammack , and Indiana\xe2\x80\x99s Erin Houchin all vying for the nod. Assuming she passes confirmation, Stefanik\xe2\x80\x99s assignment to the East River will create a vacancy early next year in her upstate New York district: Once a competitive seat, New York\xe2\x80\x99s 21st has become safe Republican territory because of the rural conservative drift, the latest redistricting, and the strength of Stefanik\xe2\x80\x99s incumbency.

    Democrats are giving this potential race a look, but they don\xe2\x80\x99t seem terribly inclined to make a major investment there on par with Tom Suozzi \xe2\x80\x99s New York 3rd District special election earlier this year. Now, some might argue this could be a test case for the midterms. During Trump\xe2\x80\x99s first term, he chose seven-term Congressman Tom Price of Georgia as his health and human services secretary\xe2\x80\x94only to see Price\xe2\x80\x99s once-safe suburban Republican seat, formerly occupied by Newt Gingrich , become competitive in a special election, which in turn led to the most expensive House race in history. The Democratic candidate in that special, Jon Ossoff , only lost by 3 points, a harbinger of the coming blue wave in 2018.

    Just prior to his White House appointment, Price had won reelection by a 23-point margin; Stefanik won her bid for reelection last week by 25 points. But the rural nature of this district probably makes the Stefanik seat a heavier lift than the Price seat. In any case, the potential Stefanik vacancy from Congress will be an annoyance for Johnson and the leadership team as they count votes next year. A New York special election to fill Stefanik\xe2\x80\x99s seat could test Democratic enthusiasm (or lack thereof) after such a depressing cycle. And the political climate could shift dramatically over the next six months, as voters become reacquainted with the Trump chaos.
And now to the main event\xe2\x80\xa6
The Missing Resistance & The Tao of Rahm
After the heartburn of 2004, Democrats rebuilt their party through a new set of candidates in the House, winning the midterms and paving the way for a generationally talented, constituency-realigning outsider to claim the presidency four years later. Rahm Emanuel, who helped engineer the party\xe2\x80\x99s comeback, has a few ideas about what the Democrats need to do now in order to reclaim the presidency\xe2\x80\x94with no #Resistance in sight.
PETER HAMBY
First, a post-election scene: My wife and I were taking a sunny afternoon walk through our neighborhood in Los Angeles on Sunday. We strolled along Abbot Kinney, the destination shopping strip in Venice that features all the stores you\xe2\x80\x99d expect to find on a similar street in any affluent blue city: Rag & Bone, Blue Bottle Coffee, Our Place, Aviator Nation, and so on. People were going about their day, sipping matcha, spending their money. We saw those Harris - Walz camo hats on two separate people, both well-styled men with mustaches who, I\xe2\x80\x99ll venture to guess, may not have fired a shotgun in their lives or traveled to an actual swing state to canvas before Election Day. They needed to say something , I guess.

The hats would do for now. But then we stumbled upon a scene that would make Neil Postman groan: We almost tripped over 20-something young women blocking the sidewalk, decked out in their Alo, excitedly grouped up in front of an iPhone that was suctioned to a Tesla window as they prepared to make a TikTok. It wasn\xe2\x80\x99t exactly that clip from Myanmar a few years ago, with the fitness instructor recording her dance moves for YouTube as tanks rolled in behind her on their way to a military coup. But for someone thinking constantly about what happened last Tuesday, it didn\xe2\x80\x99t feel far off. Amuse ourselves to death? Sure, why not.

I\xe2\x80\x99m not here to suggest that the United States of Venice can tell us much about the mood of the nation, even though it did resemble the rest of the country in one sense on Election Day. Even in my super-liberal precinct, Kamala Harris underperformed Joe Biden \xe2\x80\x99s 2020 margin, and Trump got three times as many votes as he did four years ago. (And this being Venice, of course, local gym rat Robert F. Kennedy Jr. got 19 votes, too).

As we learned plainly last Tuesday, much of the country has no interest in the political and cultural attitudes of snooty, affluent blue America. Democrats lost in good measure because of the party\xe2\x80\x99s connection to those attitudes. \xe2\x80\x9cLook, if you decide to do identity politics, it gives the other side permission to do identity politics,\xe2\x80\x9d said Rahm Emanuel , the U.S. ambassador to Japan and cutthroat Democratic strategist, who called me up from Tokyo today. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd guess what? Our identity politics do not add up to a majority. Their identities are a majority, not ours. Identity politics, on its face, is strategically bankrupt. It\xe2\x80\x99s nuts!\xe2\x80\x9d

I\xe2\x80\x99ve learned one thing, though, just from existing in one of America\xe2\x80\x99s progressive capitals the last few days: Democrats are not even close to reacting, or revolting, or resisting like they did in the aftermath of 2016. Back then, Trump\xe2\x80\x99s first election was a fluke, a once-in-a-lifetime warping of the space-time continuum, with blame resting at the feet of Mark Zuckerberg , or Vladimir Putin , or James Comey . The legendary Women\xe2\x80\x99s March on Washington, marked by its iconic pink pussy hats, was being organized on Facebook the day after Trump won. Getting involved was cool.

This time around? The organizer of the march, Teresa Shook , told The Washington Post she\xe2\x80\x99s taking a pass. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s not the same feeling,\xe2\x80\x9d Shook told the paper . \xe2\x80\x9cUsually I get angry and mad and I have to do something about it. But right now I\xe2\x80\x99m feeling like I want to curl up in the fetal position. It feels way more devastating today. Way more hopeless.\xe2\x80\x9d
A New Kind of Reckoning
It\xe2\x80\x99s not just exhausted voters. I\xe2\x80\x99m getting the same vibe from the professionals inside the Democratic Party, some of whom worked on the Harris campaign, some who worked on others. The comment I got Monday from one very- very -senior Democrat in Washington was typical: \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m planning to tune out and watch nothing, and I am a fairly highly engaged demo!\xe2\x80\x9d

I considered that I could be overstating things. So I gut-checked my observations about the left on Monday with some anti-Trump media hosts who were #Resistance favorites: Jon Favreau and Tommy Vietor at Crooked Media, and Tim Miller at The Bulwark. The Pod Save America guys told me their post-election episodes are outperforming their pre-election episodes, and Miller told me that The Bulwark\xe2\x80\x99s most downloaded podcast ever dropped the day after the election. \xe2\x80\x9cWe have seen paid subscriber growth far outpace cancels post-election,\xe2\x80\x9d Miller told me. On the flip side, there\xe2\x80\x99s MSNBC, which has been awash in liberal tears since Trump\xe2\x80\x99s win and has seen its ratings drop .

Whatever the clicks and downloads look like, no Democrat is under the illusion that their voters are just going to take a quick breather, sign up for new volunteer shifts, and get back in the fight . Levar Stoney , the Democratic mayor of Richmond, who is running to be Virginia\xe2\x80\x99s next lieutenant governor, told me something similar about the people he spoke to over the weekend at his barbershop, Dave\xe2\x80\x99s. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen Hillary lost in 2016, I felt anger in the streets. People were ready to be the resistance. Now I just hear a lot of disbelief and mourning. I hear people wanting to look inward, to get our shit together.\xe2\x80\x9d

Maybe the sentiment will pass after the holidays, after a mental health break. Or maybe a new version of the #Resistance will rear its head next year, after Trump takes office and events arise . But for now, the fatigue I\xe2\x80\x99m hearing from Democrats plays right into Trump\xe2\x80\x99s hands. When better to run up the score than when the other side isn\xe2\x80\x99t playing defense?

The obituaries for Harris\xe2\x80\x99s campaign, and what went wrong, are piling up. (Yesterday, my colleague John Heilemann wrote up a convenient taxonomy of the blame game that you can read here .) Joe Biden was a massive drag. Harris didn\xe2\x80\x99t break with him hard enough. Prices mattered more than democracy. The party is too consumed by identity politics and whatever radical chic opinions their woke young staffers and media allies care about. Dems forgot how to communicate with people who don\xe2\x80\x99t have college degrees and they are discombobulated about pronoun culture. Voters, meanwhile, don\xe2\x80\x99t see Trump as the menace that Democrats and never-Trump Republicans insist he is\xe2\x80\x94a dynamic I picked up on during my campaign travels this fall.

Democrats are also starting to confront something uncomfortable: Those ads highlighting Harris\xe2\x80\x99s past support for taxpayer-funded transgender prison surgeries? They were a killer. For proof, just look at the 538 chart tracking Harris\xe2\x80\x99s favorable ratings, which were rising until October 1, when she was high on vibes, and then started to sink the very week Trump and his MAGA allies started putting serious money behind those trans ads, which were absolutely inescapable on TV and streaming in the closing weeks. \xe2\x80\x9cI talked to so many people in the barbershop who asked about that ad,\xe2\x80\x9d Stoney told me. \xe2\x80\x9cI get that it can be difficult to talk about those issues, but there was room for the campaign to respond to them. Why didn\xe2\x80\x99t they? Because once you don\xe2\x80\x99t respond, people start to believe it\xe2\x80\x99s true.\xe2\x80\x9d

Stoney reminded me that his fellow Richmonder, Trump campaign manager and former Swift Boat Veterans for Truth dark genius Chris LaCivita , was one of the strategists behind those ads. That \xe2\x80\x9904 Kerry race has been at the top of my mind since Tuesday. After Kerry\xe2\x80\x99s loss, Dems were shell-shocked. That race was supposed to be retribution for the stolen election of 2000, and the Iraq fiasco. But Dems lost after being tarred as the party of elites, unpatriotic softies, out-of-touch Hollywood puppets. The loss was so bad and so total that it felt existential. Democrats and urban liberals blamed red state voters as rubes for voting against their interests ( What\xe2\x80\x99s The Matter With Kansas? ). They leaned into a new media ecosystem (the blogosphere!). D.N.C. chairman Howard Dean launched a \xe2\x80\x9c50-state strategy\xe2\x80\x9d to figure out how to connect with Bubbas in pickup trucks. Two of the putative 2008 presidential hopefuls were Southern white dudes, John Edwards and Mark Warner .

But the path back to power was in no way predictable. George W. Bush stumbled early into his second term, with a civil war exploding in Iraq and Hurricane Katrina exposing his ineptitude. The guy who eventually became the next Democratic president, Barack Hussein Obama , was very much not a Southern white dude and wasn\xe2\x80\x99t interested in the party\xe2\x80\x99s pecking order or waiting his turn . But before that, there were the midterms of 2006, which resulted in sweeping Democratic gains up and down the ballot and a takeover of Congress. The 2026 midterms have to be the next target for despondent Democrats, even if voters out there in the country aren\xe2\x80\x99t very excited about what the party is selling.

Rahm, who helped engineer the party\xe2\x80\x99s comeback in 2006 as head of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, recruited candidates who didn\xe2\x80\x99t fit neatly into the usual Jell-O mold. \xe2\x80\x8b\xe2\x80\x9cI remember distinctly being told when I was reaching out to candidates that these aren\xe2\x80\x99t real Democrats ,\xe2\x80\x9d he told me Monday. \xe2\x80\x9cBut that was the whole premise! If you are going to flip a district, you have to recruit people who are going to make a cultural connection in that district. The goal wasn\xe2\x80\x99t to make you feel comfortable in Washington or the Upper West Side or Beverly Hills. It\xe2\x80\x99s about winning, you dummies.\xe2\x80\x9d
Punching Left
Democrats, Rahm continued, need to put aside purity tests and find candidates who aren\xe2\x80\x99t afraid to piss off the language police and progressive scolds that seem to spook so many operatives in the party. Emanuel sounded exasperated when he recalled how progressives savaged Biden after he used the term \xe2\x80\x9cillegal alien\xe2\x80\x9d in his State of the Union address earlier this year, a comment he was forced to apologize for. \xe2\x80\x9cYou are illegally coming across the border, and you are an immigrant,\xe2\x80\x9d said Emanuel. \xe2\x80\x9cI mean, come on.\xe2\x80\x9d

Emanuel said that Democrats need to remind voters that they are the party of the little guy, standing up to big banks and billionaires. He also put a blunter edge on a sentiment I\xe2\x80\x99d heard a lot from pragmatic strategists in recent years: That Democrats can only win when they start punching left and pushing back against the orthodoxies that have spread from elite campuses into party committees and big-city newsrooms. Grad school concepts about identity, gender, race, or crime that are either unpopular, inscrutable, or far removed from people\xe2\x80\x99s daily lives.

That\xe2\x80\x99s one reason certain Senate candidates, including members of the \xe2\x80\x9906 class such as Jon Tester and Sherrod Brown , outran Harris in red states, he said. And it explains why winning candidates Tammy Baldwin , Ruben Gallego , and Jacky Rosen have white-knuckled their way to victory. They didn\xe2\x80\x99t let their races get nationalized, and they worked overtime to talk less about Trump and the culture wars, and more about the kitchen table. \xe2\x80\x9cWe don\xe2\x80\x99t understand that working-class people are more than just the summation of their wallet,\xe2\x80\x9d Emanuel said. \xe2\x80\x9cThey have kids that go to school. They care about public safety. Like, we are telling people crime is going down, but they go to CVS and everything is locked up and no one is working there? That doesn\xe2\x80\x99t compute, I\xe2\x80\x99m sorry. Save your academic presentations for the Aspen Ideas Festival.\xe2\x80\x9d

Punching left, of course, is more easily said than done, as Congressman Seth Moulton learned over the weekend when he spoke to The New York Times with unusual honesty about trans athletes playing in women\xe2\x80\x99s and girls sports. \xe2\x80\x9cI have two little girls, I don\xe2\x80\x99t want them getting run over on a playing field by a male or formerly male athlete, but as a Democrat I\xe2\x80\x99m supposed to be afraid to say that.\xe2\x80\x9d One of Moulton\xe2\x80\x99s staffers resigned in protest, and he was condemned by the head of the Massachusetts Democratic Party. At the same time, in polls, large majorities of the country agree with Moulton\xe2\x80\x99s take.

There\xe2\x80\x99s no simple blueprint here. The only consensus I\xe2\x80\x99ve been able to pick up on from Democrats, however vague, is that the party needs to get back to listening to voters rather than talking at them. Congresswoman Marie Gluesenkamp Perez , a flannel-wearing Democrat who won reelection last week in a southwestern Washington district that also voted for Trump, has been doing the media rounds the last few days explaining how she pulled out her race while other Democrats stumbled in what was a terrible political environment. But when I talked to her Monday, even she acknowledged there were limits to how much national Democrats could learn from her win. \xe2\x80\x9cI don\xe2\x80\x99t want to be a poster child or a cardboard cutout,\xe2\x80\x9d Gluesenkamp Perez told me. \xe2\x80\x9cIf there is a model here, it\xe2\x80\x99s just that you have to fight for where you come from, to show up and do the work. That has to be the motivator, and it\xe2\x80\x99s how you break the ideological map.\xe2\x80\x9d
